Glossary
Income Statements - One of the important financial statements used for reporting a company’s
financial performance over a given accounting period. This is also known as the Profit and Loss
Statement and focuses on revenue and expenses.
Accrual Accounting - This accounting method allows for the recording of revenue before receiving
payment for the services or goods. Essentially the revenue is recognized when the goods or services
were completed and/or distributed regardless of when the actual transaction occurred.
Net Sales - All of a company’s gross sales minus the returns, discounts, and allowances.
Cost of Sales - The total of all the costs incurred to create a service or product sold.
Gross Income - Revenue from all sources minus the cost of goods sold. This term is
interchangeable with gross profit and gross margin.
Selling, General and Administrative Expenses - This line item includes nearly all business costs
that are not attributed to performing a service or making a product and includes the general,
administrative expenses, and the selling expenses of the business.
Operating Income - measurement of the profit from operations after the deduction of wages, cost of
goods, and depreciation.
Other Income & Expenses - Income from other than normal operations of the company. An example
of this could be income on bank deposits.
Pretax Income - This is the company’s income after all operating expenses have been deducted from
the total sales and revenue. This number is calculated prior to the subtraction of income taxes.
Taxes - Mandatory contributions levied by a government entity. Can be local, regional, or national.
Net Income (after Taxes) - This accounting term describes a company’s profit after all taxes have
been paid.
Materials and Productions - Income calculated when the costs of materials and productions are
subtracted.
Financial Statements 101

-- 1 of 3 --

Marketing and Administrative - Income calculated minus the marketing and administrative costs of
the business over and given period.
Research and Development Expenses - Income of a company minus and research and
development expenses
Gross Profit - profit calculated after deducting all the costs associated with making and selling
products or providing services.
Gross Profit Margin - Calculation of the amount of money left from product sales after accounting for
the cost of goods sold. Gross Profit Margin = Net Sales - COGS/Net Sales
General and Administrative Costs - Costs associated with the normal running of a business and
may include rent, utilities, insurance payments, wages and salaries, and marketing and advertising
costs.
Earnings Before Interest and Taxes (EBIT) - This indicator of company profitability is calculated as
the revenue minus expenses with the exclusion of interest and tax. Other terms used to describe
EBIT include operating profit, operating earning, and profit before interest and taxes.
Shareholder Equity - The owner’s leftover assets after all debts have been paid.
Assets - Any resource with an economic value that a company or owns with a future expectation of
benefit. Liabilities + Shareholder’s Equity = Asset.
Current Assets - Assets having a lifespan of one year or less which can be easily converted to cash
Non-Current Assets - Also known as a fixed asset these are intended to take one year or longer to
be able to liquify or turn into cash. These assets do undergo depreciation.
Receivables - outstanding invoices between the business and its customer base.
Prepaid Expenses - This asset is the result of making a payment on goods or services that will be
received in the future. On the income statement these are initially recorded as an asset.
Intangible Assets - Any asset that is not physical. Examples of this include brand recognition,
goodwill and different types of intellectual property (patents, trademarks, copyrights, etc…).
Liabilities - Something owed to someone else. In business this is typically some sum of
money. Liabilities are settled over time and can include loans, mortgages, bonds, deferred revenue,
accounts payable, etc…

-- 2 of 3 --

Equity - The money that would be returned to all shareholders in the case of liquidation when all
assets were liquidated and debt paid.
Retained Earnings - Portion of company profits held or saved for future use.
Financial Ratio Analysis - Comparing two or more items of data from a company’s financial
statements.
Working capital = Current assets-Current liabilities. The difference between current assets and
current liabilities.
Debt-to-equity ratios = Total liabilities/total Shareholders Equity. This ratio evaluates financial
leverage and indicates the amount of shareholder equity and debt used to finance company assets.
Cash Flow Statement - This statement shows the amount of cash and cash equivalents the leaves
and enters a company.
Cash from operating activities - The amount of money brought in from regular and ongoing
business activities.
Cash from investing activities - The amount of money flowing into a company from its investing
activities including the sale of securities or assets as well as the purchase of physical assets, or
investments in securities.
Cash from financing activities - Cash flow used to fund the company and may include debt and
equity transactions, or dividends.
Direct Method Cash Flow Statement - adding up all the various types of cash payment and receipts
to include cash paid to suppliers, cash receipts from customers, and cash paid out in salaries.
Indirect Method Cash Flow Statement - Calculation of cash flow made by the adjustment of net
income by adding or subtracting the differences resulting from the non-cash transactions.
